video_title: The $88 Trillion Tokenization Shift Has Started with DTCC & BCG
gist: In a 51 Insights webinar, DTCC's Nadine Shakar and BCG's Christian Schmidt and Roy Shukri detail DTCC's move from tokenization pilots to live production, framing tokenization as a multi-decade infrastructure transition (not an innovation theme) that BCG projects could tokenize ~$88T (16% of real-world assets) by ~2035 and put ~35% of bank profits under scrutiny, with collateral/repo as the first-scaling use case and DTCC positioning as a neutral multi-chain 'structural orchestrator.'

themes.theme: DTCC moves from pilots to live production

insights.claim: DTCC, acting on an SEC no-action letter granted in December 2025, is starting to tokenize assets it holds in custody with live real trades beginning in July 2025 and a broader launch in October, explicitly not another pilot.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC global head of tokenization/digital assets
insights.so_what: The largest US securities depository going live with real tokenized settlement is the concrete catalyst that turns tokenization from thesis to tradable infrastructure; watch the October launch as the real inflection.
insights.quote: So we are done with PC's, we're done with experiments. This is about real world assets moving on real rails to make a real difference

insights.claim: DTCC's named list of 50 firms (including BlackRock, JP Morgan, Goldman, State Street) is not exclusive; because DTCC is owned and governed by its participants, tokenization access is open to all participants and a wide vendor ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Firms not on the headline list of 50 are not locked out; the participant-owned utility model means broad access, so the competitive edge is readiness rather than being named.

insights.claim: DTCC settles roughly $10 trillion a day, custodies over $100 trillion (cited as a 114 trillion depository), and the US market alone holds $150 trillion in assets and settles four quadrillion dollars of securities a year.
insights.speaker: host / Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: The scale of legacy volumes explains why new tokenized rails must supplement rather than replace existing plumbing and why full migration is a multi-decade process.
insights.quote: there's $150 trillion in assets and we settle four quadrillion dollars worth of securities a year

insights.claim: DTCC keeps digital shares and traditional shares under one CUSIP by design so holdings stay reconciled (a thousand shares stays a thousand shares) and liquidity is not fragmented across tokenized and traditional forms.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: The single-CUSIP design is the anti-fragmentation control that lets institutions adopt tokenization without splitting liquidity pools, a key structural difference from crypto-native issuance.
insights.quote: the digital shares and the traditional shares share one CQIP and that was done on purpose by design

insights.claim: Shakar frames the 'holy trinity' now converging for tokenization as institutional infrastructure (DTCC, NASDAQ, NYSE), digital cash (stablecoins/tokenized deposits), and regulatory clarity, with the late-2024 regulatory unlock and the 2025 DTCC no-action letter as the 'watershed moment.'
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Regulatory clarity was the missing leg after a decade of stalled experiments; its arrival is what distinguishes this cycle and unlocks institutional capital sitting on the sidelines.
insights.quote: the 25 no action letter for DTCC was honestly a watershed moment

insights.claim: BCG argues tokenization is an 'infrastructure transition' analogous to telecom's shift from circuit-switched to packet-switched networks or 2G-to-5G, transitions that in telecom took 20-plus years, implying a multi-year, possibly multi-decade migration.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG global banking/CIB lead
insights.so_what: Framing it as infrastructure (not a fad) means the strategic question for banks is timing and who pays, not whether it happens; positions for a slow-then-fast adoption curve.
insights.quote: there are some analogies to other industries like the telecommunications industry when it's you know switched from circuit switch to packet switch

insights.claim: Schmidt says the pattern for such transitions is that markets overestimate impact in the short term and underestimate it in the long term, so there is no reason for banks to panic now but strong reason to set strategy on where to invest.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: Sets expectations against near-term hype disappointment while flagging long-term underappreciation, a cue to build optionality rather than chase immediate returns.
insights.quote: we uh overestimate them in the short term but we underestimate them in the long term

insights.claim: BCG models that up to ~30-35% of bank profits are under scrutiny/at stake from tokenization out to 2033-2035, presented as scenario modeling rather than a firm forecast.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG / host
insights.so_what: A third of bank profit pools being contestable is the number that forces boards to treat this as strategic; use it to gauge which business lines (trading, transaction banking, NIM) are most exposed.
insights.quote: almost 35% of profit is under scrutiny until 2035

insights.claim: BCG's headline $88 trillion tokenized real-world-asset figure rests on the assumption that 16% of all real-world assets are tokenized within ~10 years via exponential growth, differentiated by asset class (low for native equities, higher for commodities and bonds).
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: The bull case hinges on a single 16% penetration assumption; investors should stress-test it (BCG itself flags 8% as a plausible alternative) rather than treat $88T as certain.
insights.quote: the the key assumption was like it's 16% of all the real world assets are tokenized by in 10 years from now

insights.claim: Schmidt himself hedges the $88T number, saying to 'take it with a grain of salt,' noting the 16% could be 8%, and that its real value is as a scenario base for bespoke company-level strategy rather than an absolute truth.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: The report's own author downplaying its headline number is a signal to discount consultancy TAM figures and focus on directional momentum instead of the projection.
insights.quote: So I would take it with a grain of salt. It's not the absolute truth but it's simply you know 16% it's not unthinkable.

insights.claim: Current market sizes cited: cryptocurrency roughly $3 trillion (down from end of last year), tokenized money about $300 billion (roughly the stablecoin market cap), and tokenized real-world assets only about $330 billion against a ~$300 trillion pool of real-world assets.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: Tokenized RWAs at ~$330B are a rounding error versus the $300T addressable pool, quantifying how early the transition is and the runway if penetration rises.
insights.quote: Tokenized money is like 300 billion. That's more or less the stable coin market cap. And then tokenized real world assets is at 30 only 330 billion

insights.claim: Shakar contrasts BCG's ~$80-88T projection with Citi's roughly $7 trillion by 2030 estimate but says the exact number doesn't matter; she'd be happy with $1 trillion in the next couple of years because momentum, not the figure, drives adoption.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: The operator closest to the plumbing anchors near-term expectations far below the $88T headline, a reality check on timing for anyone trading the theme.
insights.quote: Listen, I' I'd be happy with a trillion in the next couple of years.

insights.claim: Shukri argues concentrated markets like US Treasury repo (about $1 trillion-plus) and daily derivative margin could transition very quickly because 15-20 counterparties drive huge volume, so a few players agreeing can flip a market.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG capital markets lead North America
insights.so_what: Concentrated wholesale markets are the fast-migration candidates; watch repo and derivative margin for near-term tokenization tipping points rather than fragmented retail assets.
insights.quote: many of them are pretty concentrated markets where 15 to 20 counterparties drive a huge amount of volume

insights.claim: BCG segments the ecosystem into three buckets: cryptocurrencies, tokenized money (tokenized deposits, stablecoins, CBDCs, synthetic CBDCs, tokenized money market funds), and tokenized real-world assets (equities, fixed income, commodities, real estate, private capital), with securities financing/repo and derivatives margin as cross-asset 'transversal' plumbing.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: This taxonomy separates crypto from institutional tokenization and identifies where value case and adoption momentum differ, useful for mapping exposure by sub-asset class.
insights.quote: crypto tokenized money tokenized real world asset and then there are subasset classes within each of them

insights.claim: Shukri names tokenized money market funds as an interesting instrument that straddles between real-world assets and tokenized money, and cites Fnality's work as an example of a synthetic CBDC.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Tokenized MMFs are a dual-use instrument (yield-bearing collateral and settlement money), a category to watch as a bridge product between the security and cash legs.

insights.claim: Board-level conversations over the past 18-24 months shifted from conflating crypto/DLT/tokenization to treating tokenization as a distinct institutional-grade technology, which Shukri says removed a major barrier to momentum.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: The de-conflation of crypto from tokenization is what made boards willing to fund it; a sentiment shift that underpins the current institutional wave.
insights.quote: many of the board members and even senior management would conflate crypto and distributed ledger technology and tokenization

themes.theme: Where value unlocks first: collateral, asset management, trading

insights.claim: Shakar calls collateral the biggest 'super app' for tokenization because moving money at network speed enables real-time mark-to-market, reducing capital, lowering cost of capital and making financing more affordable.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Collateral mobility is the clearest near-term ROI use case; intraday liquidity and freed-up capital are the tangible benefits to watch as DTCC scales it first.
insights.quote: the biggest super app right now uh for uh for tokenization is around collateral

insights.claim: In the lightning round, Shukri bets collateral and repo scale before fund distribution, citing Shakar's existing work, high value to unlock, and the small number of participants in that ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Direct sequencing call: position for collateral/repo tokenization ahead of tokenized fund distribution when timing the theme.

insights.claim: Schmidt argues asset management benefits most because tokenization expands the universe of manageable assets, bringing previously unmanaged holdings and hard-to-access classes (tokenized real estate, commodities, private credit) into managed, more-easily-distributed wrappers.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: Asset managers gain AUM expansion and new distribution channels; a structural tailwind for the sector even as fee margins stay competitive.
insights.quote: the asset universe that can be managed it's going to increase

insights.claim: For trading, Schmidt's hypothesis is that trading frequency rises while margins come under pressure (roughly offsetting), but return on equity could jump sharply because far less equity and liquidity are needed to run the business.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: The trading upside is capital efficiency (higher ROE via lower equity/liquidity needs), not revenue growth, reframing how to value tokenization's impact on trading desks.
insights.quote: there might be a big jump in return on equity in trading because simply you need less equity and less liquidity

insights.claim: Shukri says tokenization can create liquidity and better price discovery in illiquid pockets of fixed income and private capital, and improve fund-management transparency via clear traceability and lineage from fund to underlying asset.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Private capital and illiquid fixed income are where tokenization's liquidity premium is largest; a value driver for investors seeking access and price discovery in those classes.

insights.claim: Shakar cautions the cost-benefit will be slower to materialize because it requires a full retooling of front, middle and back office, but firms that rethink their distribution and operating model first will see an uptick much faster.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: First-mover advantage accrues to firms that redesign distribution/operating models early; back-office cost savings lag, so watch commercial-model reinvention as the leading indicator.

insights.claim: DTCC is pursuing a multi-chain strategy (Canton and Stellar among the first, plus its own EVM-based chain) rather than picking a single winning chain, building a harmonization layer on top to move assets across chains and reconcile how each chain treats data.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: No single chain wins; the value accrues to the neutral interoperability/data-harmonization layer, so bet on standards-setters rather than any one blockchain token.
insights.quote: I'm less concerned about how many chains we're operating on as long as we've got good interoperability standards, good data standards

insights.claim: Both Shakar and Shukri assert the future is a multi-chain ecosystem, not one chain that controls all assets, though with some industry consolidation and strong shared standards for data movement replacing today's reconciliation needs.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG / Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Rejects the winner-take-all chain thesis; the differentiator versus today's infrastructure is standardized interoperability that reduces reconciliation, favoring orchestration plays.
insights.quote: the future is not one chain that controls all of the assets, but it's really a multi-chain state of the world

insights.claim: DTCC positions itself as a neutral 'structural orchestrator' and record-keeper that does not trade or service client accounts and refuses to pick chain winners or losers, extending its record-keeper role into the digital age.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: DTCC's neutrality (no competition with participants) is its moat as the orchestration layer; the 'structural orchestrator' role is where durable infrastructure value concentrates.
insights.quote: I love that word structural orchestrator and that is that is what we're striving to be like digital financial market infrastructure over time.

insights.claim: Schmidt argues clients don't care whether settlement uses a stablecoin, tokenized deposit or CBDC, so the institutions that can shield this technical complexity from clients will win.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: Competitive advantage shifts to complexity-abstraction and orchestration, not the underlying instrument choice, a lens for picking winners among intermediaries.
insights.quote: those institution that can shield all this complexity from clients will actually win

insights.claim: BCG and DTCC (via DTCClear) built a risk taxonomy identifying novel risks from tokenization (cyber, quantum) and argue programmability lets risk/compliance checks like AML whitelisting/blacklisting be embedded directly in smart contract code rather than run as manual post-transaction processes.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: 'Risk by design' embedded in code is a structural efficiency gain but introduces cyber/quantum/smart-contract exposures; a new risk surface for banks and their auditors to price.
insights.quote: a lot of the risk processes that are done offline today can now be integrated within code

insights.claim: Schmidt flags an unresolved problem that encoding risk management removes the discretion humans historically applied, especially in crises where judgment is needed, and says how to handle that is not yet solved.
insights.speaker: Christian Schmidt, BCG
insights.so_what: Hard-coded risk controls could behave badly in tail events where discretion matters; an open governance risk investors should track as tokenized markets scale.
insights.quote: especially in in situations of crisis right where you need to have discretion... it's put in code and I think that's something which is not completely solved yet

insights.claim: Shakar says DTCC tokens are 'compliance aware tokens' into which firms can embed their own risk management and smart contracts, and that 24/7 tradability lets institutions cover exposure and move positions when a big event hits over a weekend.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: 24/7 tokenized venues plus programmable compliance can reduce weekend/gap risk that today's closed markets impose, a genuine risk-management benefit for holders.
insights.quote: the DTCC tokens are what we call compliance aware tokens

insights.claim: Shakar says the binding constraint is no longer infrastructure, regulation or digital cash but firms' state of readiness, noting a decade of 'innovation by press release' left many now stepping back to ask whether they have proper wallet management, trading and risk-management infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Institutional readiness, not technology or rules, is the current bottleneck; firms with real wallet/risk infrastructure will separate from those that only issued announcements.
insights.quote: a lot of a lot of innovation has been done via press release over these years

insights.claim: Shakar warns the 'close follower' posture many cautious institutions adopt is risky because the space is moving fast enough that the gap between early movers and followers could become tough to catch up.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Fast-follower strategy may fail here; the widening early-mover gap argues for at least no-regret positioning now rather than waiting.
insights.quote: there's going to be a gap between the people that start um in that close follower that could be could be a bit tough to to to catch up

insights.claim: Shukri recommends every major financial institution treat tokenization as a top-10 board priority by defining a 5-10 year north star, making 'no regret' investments/partnerships today, deciding build-vs-partner for core tech stack, and systematically monitoring market tipping points to accelerate when adoption shifts.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: A concrete strategic playbook: the actionable question is not 'whether' but 'which no-regret moves and which tipping points to monitor,' useful for evaluating which banks are executing.
insights.quote: The question they're trying to figure out is what are the no regret moves, investments, partnership use cases that I should focus on.

insights.claim: Shukri says a ~$50 billion US regional bank should be a follower not a leader and rightly keeps tokenization off its top-five list, while it is now a top-10 priority for every major global systemically important bank, a shift he says did not exist two years ago.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Adoption strategy should be sized to a bank's systemic role; edge lies with large GSIBs, and regional banks are rational adopters-not-builders, informing which names lead.
insights.quote: if you are a $50 billion regional small bank in the US, is this topic on your top five list of the management team? Probably not

insights.claim: Shukri advises small regional banks not to issue their own stablecoin but to join industry consortiums to mutualize adoption cost, and to gate tokenized-deposit investment on whether their client base actually moves money cross-border.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Clear build-vs-join guidance: consortium participation over proprietary stablecoins for small banks, with cross-border client flow as the trigger for tokenized-deposit spend.
insights.quote: if you're a very small regional bank, it in our mind, it doesn't make any sense to issue your own stable coin

insights.claim: Shakar cites live evidence the rails are being built: a coalition of banks launching a tokenized deposit platform (reported that morning in the Wall Street Journal) and reported moves by Visa, Mastercard and Stripe on stablecoins.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Payment giants and bank coalitions building stablecoin/tokenized-deposit ramps signals the cash-leg infrastructure maturing in parallel, a prerequisite for RWA settlement to scale.
insights.quote: the likes of Visa, Mastercard, and Stripe are doing the same on stable coins

insights.claim: Shakar warns small banks are chasing tokenization out of FOMO ('JP Morgan is doing that or BlackRock is doing this') without a real business case, and stresses global businesses should lean to stablecoins while local businesses lean to tokenized deposits.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: FOMO-driven adoption without a business case is a red flag; the stablecoin-vs-tokenized-deposit choice should map to whether a bank's business is global or local.
insights.quote: not just FOMO

insights.claim: On who owns the ALM problem if stablecoins become the settlement layer, Shukri says the stablecoin issuer owns interest-rate-risk management on the reserves, while AML obligations are shared across issuers and the banks/distribution partners providing wallet access, per the GENIUS and CLARITY acts.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Clarifies liability allocation in stablecoin settlement: reserve interest-rate risk sits with issuers, AML/KYC is a shared duty, relevant for assessing stablecoin issuer and distributor risk.
insights.quote: the stable coin issuer will own the interest rate risk management on the reserves

insights.claim: Shukri notes a bank's stablecoin engagement may start as a defensive move but its perspective could shift rapidly if it sees significant stablecoin adoption in Asian commerce or by a large e-commerce player, a key tipping point to monitor.
insights.speaker: Roy Shukri, BCG
insights.so_what: Watch Asian commerce and large e-commerce stablecoin adoption as the trigger that flips banks from defensive to offensive stablecoin strategies.
insights.quote: if they see a significant adoption in Asian commerce or with one of the large uh e-commerce players then their perspective could change quite rapidly

insights.claim: Shakar rejects atomic (real-time gross) settlement demands, noting DTCC nets 98% of trades and there is not enough money on the planet to fund gross settlement of all flows, so tokenization must preserve netting, clearing and existing efficiencies.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: A pointed pushback on the crypto ideal of atomic settlement; netting stays essential, meaning tokenized market structure will look more hybrid than crypto maximalists expect.
insights.quote: we are so efficient that we net 98% of our trades. So there's not enough money on the planet that would allow us to take all that money in settlement and realm gross uh settlement

insights.claim: Shakar stresses the new tokenized infrastructure supplements and enhances rather than replaces existing plumbing, likening the transition to 'driving on the autobahn and changing the wheels at the same time' after 55 years building the current system.
insights.speaker: Nadine Shakar, DTCC
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations that legacy and tokenized rails coexist for years; a caution against pricing in wholesale replacement of incumbents.
insights.quote: It's really like driving on the autobon and changing the wheels at the same time.
